name: UK Waterfall Photography: A Complete Field Guide 2026

step.name: Check rainfall and timing the week before
step.text: Look at rainfall over upstream catchment for past week. 2-3 days after sustained rain is sweet spot. Check forecast for overcast bright. Avoid direct sun days. If conditions wrong, postpone.

step.name: Commit to one shot type before you leave
step.text: Pick one of four shot types — full-fall classic, intimate cascade detail, environmental wide, layered foreground-stream. Pack lens accordingly. Polariser always. ND only if going beyond 1-second in bright light.

step.name: Arrive early, scout safely, work the surround
step.text: 60-90 minutes early. Walk location, find positions others don't reach, test wet-rock surfaces. Look at surround as much as falls — moss, fern, foliage, gorge geometry, sky.

step.name: Compose, focus, fit polariser first
step.text: Tripod low and stable. Compose without filters. Manual focus on static rock edge. Reference frame at base ISO, f/8-f/11. Then fit polariser. Rotate to maximise rock saturation, minimise water glare. Polariser typically buys 1.5-2 stops light reduction.

step.name: Add ND only if you still need shutter time
step.text: Check shutter speed with polariser fitted. If 1/4-2 seconds, no ND needed. If longer exposure wanted, add 3-stop or 6-stop. 10-stop rarely needed for UK waterfalls. Use Bulb for over 30 seconds.

step.name: Bracket shutter speeds and clean the lens
step.text: Three frames at different shutter speeds: 1/4s, 1s, 4s. Keeper usually middle range. Between frames check front element with microfibre and rocket blower. Spray lands on lens within minutes.

step.name: Cull same day, edit lightly
step.text: Cull on import. From three bracketed exposures, one is keeper. Expect 4-8 keepers from 90-minute session. Edit next day. Lift shadows on rock, gentle clarity, hold saturation back on greens (polariser already saturated them). Don't push water to pure white.
